parts.context: A conversation between a customer and a travel agent at a travel agency.
parts.audio_script:
Agent: Good morning, Wanderlust Travel, this is Sarah speaking. How can I help you today?
Customer: Oh, hi. Um, I was hoping to book a train ticket to Manchester, please.
Agent: Certainly. May I have your name, please?
Customer: Yes, it’s Michael... Michael Turner.
Agent: Thank you, Mr. Turner. And when would you like to travel?
Customer: Uh, I’d prefer to go on Tuesday, if possible. Is that available?
Agent: Let me check... Yes, there are several trains on Tuesday. Do you have a preferred time?
Customer: Hmm, I think late morning would be best. Maybe around 11?
Agent: There’s a train at 11:15 AM. Would that suit you?
Customer: Yes, that’s perfect.
Agent: Great. Is it just a one-way ticket, or do you need a return as well?
Customer: Oh, I’ll need a return ticket. I’ll be coming back on Friday.
Agent: Friday, okay. Would you like to return in the morning or afternoon?
Customer: Afternoon, please. I’m not sure exactly what time, but after lunch would be ideal.
Agent: There’s a train at 2:30 PM on Friday. Shall I book that for you?
Customer: Yes, please.
Agent: Alright. So, that’s an outbound journey on Tuesday at 11:15 AM and a return on Friday at 2:30 PM. Is that correct?
Customer: Yes, that’s right.
Agent: May I ask if you have any railcards or discounts?
Customer: No, I don’t have any.
Agent: Okay. The total for a standard return ticket is £54. Would you like to reserve a seat?
Customer: Um, yes, if that’s possible. I’d prefer a window seat, if you have one.
Agent: Let me check... Yes, I can reserve a window seat for both journeys. Do you have any luggage?
Customer: Just one suitcase.
Agent: That’s fine. There’s no extra charge for one suitcase. Would you like travel insurance?
Customer: No, I don’t think so, thanks.
Agent: Alright. How would you like to pay?
Customer: By credit card, please.
Agent: Could you read out the card number for me?
Customer: Sure, it’s 4929 1234 5678 9012.
Agent: Thank you. And the expiry date?
Customer: Um, it’s 08/26.
Agent: And the security code?
Customer: 314.
Agent: Thank you. That’s all gone through. I’ll just print your tickets. Would you like them posted or will you collect them from the station?
Customer: I’ll collect them from the station, thanks.
Agent: No problem. You’ll need the booking reference, which is TQ57L. Please bring some ID when you collect your tickets.
Customer: Okay, I will. Thanks for your help.
Agent: You’re welcome, Mr. Turner. Have a pleasant journey!
Customer: Thank you. Goodbye.
Agent: Goodbye.

question_sets.instruction: Complete the notes below. Write ONE WORD ONLY for each answer.

questions.question_text: Customer wants to travel on ______.
Preferred departure time is ______.
Return journey is on ______.
Customer prefers a ______ seat.
Number of suitcases: ______.
Tickets will be collected from the ______.

question_sets.instruction: Choose the correct letter, A, B or C.

questions.question_text: How much does the return ticket cost?
What does the customer need to bring to collect the tickets?
How will the customer pay for the tickets?
Does the customer purchase travel insurance?

parts.context: A monologue by a museum guide about the new City History Museum.
parts.audio_script:
Guide: Good afternoon, everyone, and welcome to the City History Museum. My name’s Helen, and I’ll be giving you a brief introduction before you begin your visit. Now, before we start, just a quick reminder to please switch your mobile phones to silent mode. Thank you. 

The museum opened just last year, after nearly five years of planning and construction. It’s located in what used to be the old town hall, which was built in 1887. The building itself is a piece of history, and we’ve tried to preserve as much of the original architecture as possible. You’ll notice the high ceilings and stained glass windows as you walk around. 

The museum is divided into three main sections. On the ground floor, you’ll find the permanent exhibition, which covers the city’s history from Roman times right up to the present day. There are interactive displays, models, and even a reconstructed Victorian street. Children especially enjoy the hands-on activities in this area. 

Upstairs, on the first floor, we have our temporary exhibition space. At the moment, we’re showing an exhibition on the city’s role during the Second World War. This includes photographs, personal letters, and some fascinating objects donated by local residents. The exhibition will run until the end of next month, so do take a look if you’re interested in that period of history. 

The third section is the education centre, which is located in the new extension at the back of the building. Here, we run workshops for schools and community groups. If you’d like to book a place on one of our workshops, please speak to a member of staff at the information desk. 

Facilities include a café on the ground floor, which serves hot and cold drinks, sandwiches, and cakes. There’s also a gift shop next to the main entrance, where you can buy souvenirs, books, and postcards. Toilets are located on both floors, and there’s a lift for wheelchair users. 

We’re open from 10 am to 5 pm, Tuesday to Sunday. We’re closed on Mondays, except for bank holidays. Admission is free for children under 12, and there’s a small charge for adults. Guided tours are available at 11 am and 2 pm daily, and these last about an hour. If you’d like to join a tour, please sign up at the desk. 

Finally, we ask that you don’t eat or drink in the exhibition areas, and please don’t touch the displays unless they’re clearly marked as interactive. Photography is allowed, but please don’t use flash. If you have any questions during your visit, our staff will be happy to help. I hope you enjoy your time at the City History Museum. Thank you.

question_sets.instruction: Match each area of the museum (Questions 11-15) with the correct feature (A-E) from the list below.

questions.question_text: Ground floor
First floor
Back of the building

parts.context: A conversation between two university students, Anna and Ben, and their tutor, Dr. Lee, about a group project.
parts.audio_script:
Dr. Lee: Good morning, Anna, Ben. Thanks for coming in. I wanted to check how your group project is progressing. 
Anna: Morning, Dr. Lee. Um, yes, we’ve made a start. We met last week to discuss the topic and, uh, divided up the tasks. 
Ben: Yeah, we decided to focus on renewable energy in urban areas. Anna’s looking at solar power, and I’m researching wind energy. 
Dr. Lee: That sounds promising. Have you found enough sources for your research?
Anna: Well, I’ve found quite a few articles on solar panels in cities, but some of them are a bit, um, technical. I’m not sure I understand all the data.
Dr. Lee: That’s understandable. If you need help interpreting the data, let me know. What about you, Ben?
Ben: I’ve got some case studies from Europe, but I’m struggling to find information about wind turbines in city environments, specifically. Most of the research is about rural areas.
Dr. Lee: Hmm, yes, that’s a common issue. You might want to look at government reports or, uh, planning documents. Sometimes they have more relevant information.
Ben: Oh, I hadn’t thought of that. Thanks.
Dr. Lee: And how are you planning to present your findings?
Anna: We were thinking of doing a PowerPoint presentation, with some graphs and images. Maybe a short video, too, if we have time.
Dr. Lee: That’s a good idea. Just make sure the video isn’t too long—five minutes at most. And remember to reference all your sources.
Ben: Will do. We’re meeting again tomorrow to put everything together.
Dr. Lee: Excellent. Have you decided who will do which part of the presentation?
Anna: Um, not yet. We thought we’d see what material we have first, then divide it up.
Dr. Lee: That’s sensible. And don’t forget to rehearse before the actual presentation. Practising together will help you spot any problems.
Ben: Yeah, we’ll definitely do that. Oh, and, uh, we were wondering about the marking criteria. Is it mainly based on content, or do you look at how we present as well?
Dr. Lee: Both are important. I’ll be looking at the quality of your research, how well you explain your findings, and also your teamwork and presentation skills.
Anna: Okay, that’s good to know.
Dr. Lee: One more thing—make sure you submit your slides to me at least two days before your presentation, so I can check them.
Ben: Right, we’ll do that.
Dr. Lee: Great. If you have any more questions, just send me an email. Good luck with your project!
Anna: Thanks, Dr. Lee.
Ben: Thank you. See you next week.

question_sets.instruction: Choose the correct letter, A, B or C.

questions.question_text: What is the main topic of Anna and Ben’s project?
What problem does Anna mention?
Where does Dr. Lee suggest Ben look for more information?
How long should the video in the presentation be?
When should the slides be submitted to Dr. Lee?
options: A On the day of the presentation

questions.question_text: Anna is researching ______ power.

parts.context: A university lecturer giving a talk on the importance of sleep for students.
parts.audio_script:
Lecturer: Good morning, everyone. Today, I’d like to talk about something that’s often overlooked but absolutely essential for your academic success—sleep. Now, I know many of you are busy with assignments, part-time jobs, and social activities, but getting enough sleep is just as important as studying. 

Let’s start with the basics. Most adults need between seven and nine hours of sleep each night. However, research shows that university students typically get much less—sometimes as little as five hours. This lack of sleep can have a significant impact on your concentration, memory, and even your mood. You might think you can catch up on sleep at the weekend, but unfortunately, it doesn’t really work that way. Sleep debt accumulates, and it can take several days to recover from just one night of poor sleep. 

So, why is sleep so important? Well, during sleep, your brain processes information from the day and forms new memories. This is crucial for learning. If you don’t get enough sleep, you’re more likely to forget what you’ve studied. In fact, studies have shown that students who sleep well before an exam perform better than those who stay up late revising. 

Now, I often hear students say they have trouble falling asleep. There are several reasons for this. Stress is a big factor, of course, but so is the use of electronic devices late at night. The blue light from screens can interfere with your body’s production of melatonin, the hormone that helps you sleep. My advice is to switch off your phone or laptop at least an hour before bed. Try reading a book or listening to music instead. 

Another tip is to keep a regular sleep schedule. Going to bed and getting up at the same time every day—even at weekends—helps your body get into a rhythm. Also, avoid caffeine in the evening, as it can stay in your system for several hours. And, uh, make sure your bedroom is quiet and dark. If noise is a problem, you might want to use earplugs. 

Finally, if you find that you’re still tired during the day, it’s okay to take a short nap—about 20 minutes is ideal. But don’t nap for too long, or you’ll find it harder to sleep at night. 

In summary, getting enough sleep is vital for your health and your studies. Make it a priority, and you’ll find you have more energy, better focus, and, hopefully, better grades. Thank you for listening.

question_sets.instruction: Choose the correct letter, A, B or C.

questions.question_text: How many hours of sleep do most adults need each night?
What is a common effect of lack of sleep?
What does the lecturer recommend before going to bed?
What can help your body get into a sleep rhythm?
How long should a daytime nap be?

question_sets.instruction: Complete the sentences below. Write NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS AND/OR A NUMBER for each answer.

questions.question_text: Sleep helps the brain to form new ______.

passages.title: The Evolution of Urban Green Spaces
passages.content:
Urban green spaces, encompassing parks, gardens, and natural reserves within city boundaries, have undergone significant transformation since the onset of industrialization. In the nineteenth century, rapid urbanization led to the proliferation of densely populated cities, often characterized by poor air quality and limited access to natural environments. Early urban planners, influenced by the burgeoning public health movement, advocated for the creation of parks as a means to counteract the deleterious effects of industrial life. Notably, Frederick Law Olmsted, the designer of New York’s Central Park, argued that green spaces were essential for both physical and psychological well-being. His vision was predicated on the belief that exposure to nature could mitigate the stresses of urban living and foster social cohesion among diverse populations.

Throughout the twentieth century, the function and design of urban green spaces evolved in response to changing societal needs and environmental awareness. The post-war era witnessed a shift towards multifunctional landscapes, integrating recreational facilities, playgrounds, and sports fields. This period also saw the emergence of the ecological park, which prioritized biodiversity and habitat conservation alongside human recreation. The integration of ecological principles into urban planning was further propelled by the environmental movement of the 1960s and 1970s, which highlighted the importance of preserving natural habitats within metropolitan areas.

In recent decades, the role of urban green spaces has expanded to encompass a broader array of ecosystem services. These include air and water purification, urban heat island mitigation, and the promotion of mental health. Numerous studies have demonstrated that proximity to green spaces is correlated with reduced levels of stress, improved cognitive function, and lower incidence of certain chronic diseases. Moreover, urban vegetation plays a critical role in sequestering carbon dioxide and supporting urban biodiversity, providing habitat for birds, insects, and small mammals.

Despite their recognized benefits, the equitable distribution of green spaces remains a persistent challenge. Socioeconomic disparities often dictate access to quality parks and recreational areas, with marginalized communities frequently experiencing a deficit of green infrastructure. Urban planners and policymakers are increasingly cognizant of these inequities and are implementing strategies to ensure more inclusive access. Initiatives such as community gardens, green roofs, and pocket parks are being deployed to enhance urban greenery in underserved neighborhoods.

Technological advancements have also influenced the management and design of urban green spaces. Geographic Information Systems (GIS) and remote sensing technologies enable planners to assess vegetation cover, monitor ecological health, and optimize the placement of new green infrastructure. Additionally, participatory planning processes, which engage local residents in the design and stewardship of green spaces, are gaining traction as a means to foster community ownership and ensure that parks meet the diverse needs of urban populations.

Looking forward, the integration of green spaces into urban environments is likely to become increasingly complex, as cities grapple with the dual imperatives of accommodating population growth and enhancing environmental sustainability. Climate change poses new challenges, necessitating the development of resilient landscapes capable of withstanding extreme weather events and supporting urban adaptation. Innovative approaches, such as the creation of green corridors and the restoration of urban wetlands, are being explored to bolster ecological connectivity and enhance the multifunctionality of urban landscapes.

In sum, the evolution of urban green spaces reflects broader shifts in societal values, scientific understanding, and technological capability. As cities continue to expand, the imperative to create, maintain, and equitably distribute green spaces will remain central to the pursuit of sustainable and livable urban environments.

question_sets.instruction: Do the following statements agree with the information given in the passage? Write TRUE if the statement agrees with the information, FALSE if the statement contradicts the information, or NOT GIVEN if there is no information on this.

passages.title: The Globalization of Food Systems: Impacts and Implications
passages.content:
The globalization of food systems has been one of the most profound transformations of the modern era, reshaping not only what people eat but also how food is produced, distributed, and consumed. Historically, food systems were largely localized, with communities relying on regional crops and traditional agricultural practices. However, advances in transportation, refrigeration, and trade liberalization have enabled the movement of food across vast distances, resulting in a complex web of global supply chains.

One of the most significant consequences of this shift has been the homogenization of diets. As multinational corporations expand their reach, processed foods and standardized products have become ubiquitous, often displacing indigenous crops and culinary traditions. The so-called ‘nutrition transition’—a shift from diets rich in grains, legumes, and vegetables to those high in fats, sugars, and animal products—has been observed in both developed and developing countries. While this transition has contributed to reductions in undernutrition in some regions, it has also been implicated in the rising prevalence of obesity, diabetes, and cardiovascular diseases worldwide.

The environmental ramifications of globalized food systems are equally complex. Large-scale monoculture farming, driven by the demand for uniform commodities such as soy, wheat, and palm oil, has led to deforestation, soil degradation, and loss of biodiversity. The reliance on chemical fertilizers and pesticides further exacerbates ecological harm, contaminating water sources and disrupting local ecosystems. Moreover, the carbon footprint of transporting food internationally is substantial, contributing to greenhouse gas emissions and climate change.

Proponents of globalized food systems argue that they enhance food security by increasing the availability and affordability of diverse products. International trade allows regions facing food shortages to import essential staples, mitigating the risk of famine. Furthermore, technological innovations in agriculture—such as genetically modified crops and precision farming—have boosted yields and improved resilience to pests and climate variability. However, critics contend that these benefits are unevenly distributed, with smallholder farmers and marginalized communities often excluded from the gains of globalization.

The consolidation of power within the food industry is another contentious issue. A handful of multinational corporations control significant portions of the global food supply, influencing everything from seed patents to retail pricing. This concentration can stifle competition, limit consumer choice, and undermine the livelihoods of local producers. In response, there has been a growing movement advocating for food sovereignty, which emphasizes the rights of communities to define their own food systems and prioritize local production.

Efforts to create more sustainable and equitable food systems are gaining momentum. Urban agriculture, farmers’ markets, and community-supported agriculture initiatives are reconnecting consumers with local producers and promoting agroecological practices. Policymakers are also exploring regulatory frameworks to address the negative externalities of globalized food systems, such as implementing taxes on sugary beverages or incentivizing sustainable farming methods.

In conclusion, the globalization of food systems presents a paradox: while it has facilitated unprecedented access to a variety of foods and contributed to economic development, it has also engendered significant health, environmental, and social challenges. Navigating these complexities requires a nuanced approach that balances the benefits of global integration with the imperative to protect public health, preserve cultural diversity, and ensure environmental sustainability.

question_sets.instruction: Choose the correct letter, A, B, C, or D.

passages.title: The Cognitive Science of Bilingualism: Myths and Realities
passages.content:
Bilingualism, the ability to use two or more languages proficiently, has long been a subject of fascination and debate among linguists, psychologists, and educators. For much of the twentieth century, prevailing attitudes in Western societies were ambivalent, if not outright negative, towards raising children in bilingual environments. Early research, often methodologically flawed, suggested that bilingualism might hinder cognitive development, leading to confusion or linguistic deficits. However, a growing body of empirical evidence over the past several decades has fundamentally challenged these assumptions, revealing a far more nuanced and positive picture of bilingual cognition.

One of the most persistent myths about bilingualism is that it causes language delay in children. While it is true that bilingual children may initially exhibit a smaller vocabulary in each language compared to their monolingual peers, research indicates that their total vocabulary across both languages is comparable or even superior. Moreover, bilingual individuals demonstrate remarkable cognitive flexibility, particularly in tasks requiring executive control, such as switching attention, inhibiting irrelevant information, and problem-solving. These advantages are attributed to the constant management of two linguistic systems, which exercises the brain’s executive functions.

Another area of interest concerns the so-called ‘bilingual advantage’ in cognitive aging. Several longitudinal studies have found that lifelong bilingualism is associated with a delayed onset of dementia and other age-related cognitive declines. The proposed mechanism is that the regular use of multiple languages enhances cognitive reserve, enabling the brain to compensate for neuropathological changes more effectively. However, some scholars caution that these findings may be influenced by confounding variables, such as education level, socioeconomic status, and immigration history, which are often correlated with bilingualism.

The social and cultural dimensions of bilingualism are equally complex. In many contexts, bilingualism is a marker of social capital, conferring advantages in employment, education, and cross-cultural communication. Yet, it can also be a source of stigma or marginalization, particularly for speakers of minority or immigrant languages. Educational policies vary widely, with some countries promoting bilingual education as a means of fostering integration and multiculturalism, while others prioritize monolingual instruction in the dominant language.

Technological advances have further complicated the landscape of bilingualism. The proliferation of digital media, online translation tools, and global communication platforms has facilitated exposure to multiple languages, blurring the boundaries between native and non-native speakers. At the same time, concerns have been raised about the potential erosion of minority languages and the homogenizing effects of global English.

Despite the wealth of research, significant gaps remain in our understanding of bilingual cognition. For instance, the degree to which cognitive advantages generalize across different types of bilinguals—such as simultaneous versus sequential learners, or balanced versus dominant bilinguals—remains contested. Additionally, the impact of sociolinguistic context, language proficiency, and frequency of use on cognitive outcomes is an area of ongoing investigation.

In conclusion, the cognitive science of bilingualism reveals a dynamic interplay between linguistic, cognitive, and social factors. While many myths have been dispelled, the realities of bilingual experience are shaped by a complex constellation of individual and contextual variables. As societies become increasingly multilingual, a deeper understanding of bilingualism will be essential for informing educational policy, promoting linguistic diversity, and supporting cognitive health across the lifespan.

tasks.instruction: You should spend about 20 minutes on this task. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
tasks.data_description: The table below shows the percentage of households in five different countries (Canada, Germany, Japan, Brazil, and South Africa) that owned various types of electronic devices (television, computer, and smartphone) in the years 2000, 2010, and 2020. In 2000, television ownership was highest in Canada (92%), followed by Germany (89%), Japan (85%), Brazil (70%), and South Africa (60%). By 2020, television ownership had increased slightly in all countries, with Canada and Germany both reaching 98%, Japan at 95%, Brazil at 85%, and South Africa at 80%. Computer ownership in 2000 was highest in Japan (60%), with Canada at 55%, Germany at 50%, Brazil at 20%, and South Africa at 10%. By 2020, computer ownership had risen significantly, with Japan at 90%, Canada at 88%, Germany at 85%, Brazil at 60%, and South Africa at 40%. Smartphone ownership was negligible in all countries in 2000 (all below 5%), but by 2020, it had surged: Japan (95%), Canada (92%), Germany (90%), Brazil (80%), and South Africa (75%).

tasks.instruction: You should spend about 40 minutes on this task. Write about the following topic. Give reasons for your answer and include any relevant examples from your own knowledge or experience.
tasks.prompt: Some people believe that the best way to improve public health is by increasing the number of sports facilities. Others, however, argue that this would have little effect on public health and that other measures are required. Discuss both these views and give your own opinion.
